elements.sent-2411-001: In this paper we obtain bounds on a very general class of distance-based topological indices of graphs, which includes the Wiener index, defined as the sum of the distances between all pairs of vertices of the graph, and most generalisations of the Wiener index, including the Harary index and the hyper-Wiener index.
elements.sent-2411-002: Our results imply several new bounds on well-studied topological indices, among those sharp lower bounds on the Harary index and sharp upper bounds on the hyper-Wiener index for (i) graphs of given order and size (which resolves a problem in the monograph [The Harary index of a graph, Xu, Das, Trinajsti\'{c , Springer (2015)], (ii) for $\kappa$-connected graphs, where $\kappa$ is even, (iii) for maximal outerplanar graphs and for Apollonian networks (a subclass of maximal planar graphs), and (iv) for trees in which all vertices have odd degree.}
elements.sent-2411-003: Keywords: generalised Wiener index, Harary index; hyper-Wiener index; Wiener index, multiplicative Wiener index; distance sequence.
elements.sent-2411-004: Many topological indices used in chemical and pure graph theory are based on distances between vertices.
elements.sent-2411-005: The best-known among the distance-based topological indices is the Wiener index, defined as \begin{equation}W(G) = \sum_{ \{u,v\} \subseteq V(G)} d_G(u,v),\end{equation} where $V(G)$ is the vertex set of $G$ and $d_G(u,v)$ denotes the usual shortest path distance between vertices $u$ and $v$ of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-006: Two of the most important distance-based indices besides the Wiener index are the Harary index $H(G)$, defined as \begin{equation}H(G) = \sum_{ \{u,v\} \subseteq V(G)} \frac{1}{d_G(u,v)},\end{equation} and the hyper-Wiener index $WW(G)$, defined as \begin{equation}WW(G) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{ \{u,v\} \subseteq V(G)} \big(d_G(u,v)^2 + d_G(u,) \big).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-007: Bounds on these indices in terms of graph properties or other graph invariants have been the subject of intense study in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-008: Since the extremal graphs for distance-based topological indices like the Wiener index, Harary index and hyper-Wiener index coincide in many cases, it is natural to explore ways to prove extremal results for these topological indices in a unified way.
elements.sent-2411-009: This approach lead to the study of generalised Wiener indices, (also called $Q$-indices (see, for example, \cite{BruDosGraGut2011 ), of the form \begin{equation}W_f(G) = \sum_{ \{u,v\} \subseteq V(G)} f(d_G(u,v)),\end{equation} where $f$ is a real-valued function on the set of positive integers.}
elements.sent-2411-010: For suitable choices this definition includes many well-known distance-based indices, for example the Harary index, the variable Wiener index $W_{\lambda}$, where $\lambda \in \mathbb{R -\{0\}$ defined by $W_{\lambda (G) = \sum_{ \{u,v\} \subseteq V(G) (d_G(u,v))^{\lambda}$ see, for example, \cite{HriKnoSkr2019 ), the hyper-Wiener index, the generalised hyper-Wiener index $WW_{\lambda}$, defined by $WW_{\lambda (G) = \sum_{ \{u,v\} \subseteq V(G) \frac{1 {2 (d_G(u,v)^{\lambda + d_G(u,v)^{2\lambda )$, where $\lambda \in \mathbb{R -\{0\}$ (see Tomescu, Jarmad and Kamil \cite{TomArsJam2015 ), and the Tratch, Stankevich, Zefirov index $\sum_{ \{u,v\} \subseteq V(G) \frac{1 {3 \big( d_G(u,v)+ \frac{1 {2 d_G(u,v)^2 + \frac{1 {6 d_G(u,v)^3 \big)$ (see \cite{TraStaZef1990, KleGut1999 ).}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: While the multiplicative Wiener index, $\pi(G) = \prod_{ \{u,v\} \subseteq V(G) d_G(u,v)$ cannot be expressed in the form $W_f(G)$ for any $f$, the logarithm of $\pi(G)$ can.}
elements.sent-2411-012: This unified approach has yielded numerous general bounds on $W_f$.
elements.sent-2411-013: Considering trees with a given degree sequence, it was shown in \cite{SchWagWan2012, WagWanZha2013 that the so-called greedy tree minimises $W_f$.}
elements.sent-2411-014: In \cite{DanDos2021 , extremal trees minimising $W_f$ among all trees with a given eccentric sequence, i.e., the sequence of the eccentricities of the vertices, are obtained.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Unicyclic graphs with extremal values of $W_f$ were considered in \cite{MarRod-arxiv .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Sufficient conditions for graphs in terms of $W_f$ to have vulnerability parameters (such as toughness, binding number, tenacity, integrity) at least a certain value were given in \cite{HuaLiu2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Graphs with given independence number or matching number that are extremal with respect to $W_f$ were considered in \cite{Cam2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: In recent years, sufficient conditions in terms of $W_f$ that guarantee that graphs have certain Hamiltonian properties have attracted attention, see \cite{AolLiuYuaYu2023, DenKuaWuHua2017, KuaHuaDen2016, ZhoWanLu2018, ZhoWanLu2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Other approaches to a unified treatment of topological indices on trees, that also includes some indices that were not distance-based, were taken in \cite{VukSed2018 , where a partial order on the set of trees of given order is defined, and results for indices that are increasing or decreasing with respect to this partial order are obtained.}
elements.sent-2411-021: In \cite{AndRazWag2021 , the authors presented very general conditions on topological indices under which the greedy tree is always extremal among trees with a given degree sequence.}
elements.sent-2411-022: In this paper we take an entirely different approach to distance-based topological indices by utilising properties of distance sequences.
elements.sent-2411-023: The {\em distance sequence ${\cal D (G)$ of a connected graph $G$ is the nondecreasing sequence of the distances between all unordered pairs of distinct vertices of $G$.}}
elements.sent-2411-024: The distance sequence was first considered in \cite{CasDan2019 , where it was used to prove sharp bounds on the Wiener index of the strong product of graphs.}
elements.sent-2411-025: We say that a topological index $I(G)$ is {\em distance-based if it can be expressed in the form $I(G) = g({\cal D (G))$, where $g$ is a function defined on the set of all finite sequences of positive integers.}}
elements.sent-2411-026: We say that $I$ is increasing (decreasing, nonincreasing, nondecreasing) if the function $g$, when restricted to sequences of a fixed length, is increasing (decreasing, nonincreasing, nondecreasing) in every coordinate.
elements.sent-2411-027: This definition includes as special cases the generalised Wiener index $W_f$, defined as $\sum_{ \{u,v\} \subseteq V(G) f(d_G(u,v))$, where $f$ is an increasing or decreasing real function (which in turn includes well-known topological indices such as the Harary index and the hyper-Wiener index), but also, for example, the diameter of a graph, defined as the largest of the distances between its vertices.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Our approach yields several new bounds/extremal graphs for distance-based topological indices.
elements.sent-2411-029: We determine graphs that maximise (minimise) indreasing (decreasing) distance-based indices (i) among graphs of given order and size, (ii)among $\kappa$-connected graphs of given order, where $\kappa$ is even, (iii) for $k$-trees and maximal outerplanar graphs, and (iv) trees with all vertex degrees odd.
elements.sent-2411-030: In most cases, the bounds on the Harary index, the hyper-Wiener index or the multiplicative Wiener index implied by our results are new.
elements.sent-2411-031: The result under (i) above resolves an open problem from the monograph \cite{XuDasTri2015 on the Harary index.}
elements.sent-2411-032: This paper is organised as follows.
elements.sent-2411-033: In Section \ref{section:terminology , we define the terminology and notation used in this paper.}
elements.sent-2411-034: In Section \ref{section:distance sequences we introduce the degree sequence of graphs and prove basic properties.}
elements.sent-2411-035: In the following sections we obtain extremal graphs for increasing or decreasing distance-based topological indices for various graph classes: graph of given order and size are considered in Section \ref{section:order-and-size , graphs of given connectivity are considered in Section \ref{section:connectivity , maximal $k$-degenerate graphs (which as special cases contain $k$-trees, maximal outerplanar graphs and Apollonian networks which are a subclass of maximal planar graphs) are considered in Section \ref{section:outerplanar .}}}
elements.sent-2411-036: Section \ref{section:regular-trees is on two classes of trees, regular trees and trees in which all vertices have odd degree.}
elements.sent-2411-037: If $G$ is a graph, then we denote its vertex set and edge set by $V(G)$ and $E(G)$.
elements.sent-2411-038: If $u,v$ are vertices of $G$, then we say that $u$ is a neighbour of $v$ if $uv$ is an edge of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-039: The degree ${\rm deg _G(v)$ of $v$ is the number of its neighbours.}
elements.sent-2411-040: A graph is connected, if between any two of its vertices, $u$ and $v$ say, there is a $(u,v)$-path.
elements.sent-2411-041: The distance $d_G(u,v)$ between two vertices $u$ and $v$ in a connected graph is the minimum length of a $(u,v)$-path.
elements.sent-2411-042: If $u$ is a vertex of $G$, then the eccentricity of $u$, denoted by ${\rm ecc _G(u)$, is the distance from $u$ to a vertex farthest from $u$.}
elements.sent-2411-043: If $S$ is a set of vertices of $G$, then $G-S$ denotes the graph obtained from $G$ by deleting all vertices in $S$ and edges that are incident with a vertex in $S$.
elements.sent-2411-044: If $k\in \mathbb{N}$, we say that $G$ is $k$-connected if $G$ has more than $k$ vertices, and the graph $G-S$ is connected whenever $S$ is a subset of $V(G)$ with fewer than $k$ vertices.
elements.sent-2411-045: A graph $G$ is planar if it can be embedded in the plane such that no two edges intersect.
elements.sent-2411-046: A graph is outerplanar if it can be embedded in the plane such that no two edges intersect, and every vertex is on the boundary of the outer face.
elements.sent-2411-047: A graph $G$ is maximal planar (maximal outerplanar) if it is planar (outerplanar), but after adding any edge it no longer has this property.
elements.sent-2411-049: By the $k$-th power $G^k$ of $G$ we mean the graph on the same vertex set in which two vertices are adjacent if their distance is not more than $k$.
elements.sent-2411-050: We denote the path, the cycle and the complete graph on $n$ vertices by $P_n$, $C_n$ and $K_n$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-051: Recall that the {\em distance sequence ${\cal D (G)$ of a connected graph $G$ of order $n$ is the nondecreasing sequence of the distances between all unordered pairs of distinct vertices of $G$.}}
elements.sent-2411-052: The distance sequence was first considered in \cite{CasDan2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-053: If $A=(a_1, a_2,\ldots, a_k)$ and $B=(b_1,b_2,\ldots,b_k)$ are nondecreasing sequences of integers, then we write $A\leq B$ if $a_i\leq b_i$ for $i=1,2,\ldots,k$.
elements.sent-2411-054: We write $A<B$ if $A \leq B$ but $A\neq B$.
elements.sent-2411-055: The relation $\leq$ imposes a partial order on the distance sequences of connected graphs of given order.
elements.sent-2411-056: If $I$ is an nondecreasing (nonincreasing) distance-based topological index, then clearly $I(G_1) \leq I(G_2)$ ($I(G_1) \geq I(G_2)$) whenever $G_1$ and $G_2$ are connected graphs of the same order with ${\cal D (G_1) \leq {\cal D (G_2)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-057: Hence we have the following proposition.
elements.sent-2411-058: If a graph $G_n \in {\cal G _n$ satisfies ${\cal D (G) \leq {\cal D (G_n)$ for all $G \in {\cal G _n$, then \begin{equation}I(G) \leq I(G_n) \quad \textrm{for all $G \in {\cal G}_n$}.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: If a graph $G_n \in {\cal G _n$ satisfies ${\cal D (G) < {\cal D (G_n)$ for all $G \in {\cal G _n -\{G_n\}$, then \begin{equation}I(G) < I(G_n) \quad \textrm{for all $G \in {\cal G}_n - \{G_n\}$}.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: Corresponding inequalities hold if $I$ is nonincreasing or decreasing.
elements.sent-2411-061: The following lemma is a slightly more general version of the well-known fact that $W(G) \leq W(G-v) + d_G(v)$ if $v$ is not a cut-vertex of $G$, where $d_G(v)$ is the sum of the distances between $v$ and all other vertices of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-062: If $A$ and $B$ are sequences of integers, then we denote the sequence obtained from the concatenation of $A$ and $B$ by arranging its element in nondecreasing order by $A \odot B$.
elements.sent-2411-063: Then \begin{equation}\label{eq:sequence-of-G-minus-v} {\cal D}(G) \leq {\cal D}(G-v) \odot {\cal D}_G(v),\end{equation} with equality if and only if $d_{G-v (u,w) \leq 2$ for all $u,w \in N_G(v)$.. \end{la.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Let ${\cal D '$ be the nondecreasing sequence of the distances in $G$ between all pairs of vertices in $V(G)-\{v\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-066: Since $d_G(u,w) \leq d_{G-v (u,w)$ for all $u,w \in V(G)-\{v\}$, we have ${\cal D ' \leq {\cal D (G-v)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Clearly, equality in \eqref{eq:sequence-of-G-minus-v holds if an only if $d_G(u,w) = d_{G-v (u,w)$ for all $u,w \in V(G)-\{v\}$, which in turn holds if and only if $d_G(u,w) = d_{G-v (u,w)$ for all $u,w \in N_G(v)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: The latter holds if and only $d_{G-v (u,w) \leq 2$ for all $u,w \in N_G(v)$.}
elements.section-2411-004: Graphs of given order and size
elements.sent-2411-070: In this section we consider graphs of given order and size.
elements.sent-2411-071: Sharp lower bounds on nondecreasing/increasing (or upper bounds on nonincreasing/decreasing) distance-based indices are easily derived from the fact that a connected graph of order $n$ and size $m$ has $m$ pairs of vertices at distance $1$, and the remaining $\binom{n {2 -m$ pairs have distance at least $2$.}}
elements.sent-2411-072: For example, if $f$ is an increasing function then $W_f(G) \geq m f(1) + (\binom{n {2 -m)f(2)$ for every graph of order $n$ and size $m$, with equality if and only if ${\rm diam (G) \leq 2$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-073: Sharp upper bounds on nondecreasing/increasing (or lower bounds on noninccreasing/decreasing) distance-based indices are less straightforward to obtain.
elements.sent-2411-074: For the Wiener index, Solt\'{e s \cite{Sol1991 proved that, among all connected graphs of given order $n$ and size $m$, the path-complete graph $PK_{n,m}$ (defined below) is the unique graph maximising the Wiener index.}}
elements.sent-2411-075: No sharp upper bound on the hyper-Wiener index in terms of order and size is known, except for the special case $m=n$, i.e., for unicyclic graphs (see \cite{XinZhoQi2011 ).}
elements.sent-2411-076: We add that lower and upper bounds on the hyper-Wiener index in terms of order and size that also take into account eccentricities of vertices or diameter were given in in \cite{FenLiu2012 and \cite{AlhBagRah2017 .}}
elements.sent-2411-077: In their monograph on the Harary index \cite{XuDasTri2015 , Xu, Das and Trinajsti\'{c posed the problem of determining a sharp lower bound on the Harary index in terms of order and size.}}
elements.sent-2411-078: Theorem \ref{theo:order-size below solves this problem not only for the Harary index, but for increasing distance-based topological indices.}
elements.sent-2411-079: Following Solt\'{e s \cite{Sol1991 , we define a path-complete graph as a graph obtained from the union of a path and a complete graph by joining one end of the path to one or more vertices of the complete graph.}}
elements.sent-2411-080: It is not hard to see that for given $n,m \in \mathbb{N}$ with $n-1 \leq m < \binom{n {2}$ there exists a unique path-complete graph of order $n$ and size $m$.}
elements.sent-2411-082: Using Solt\'{e s' approach, it was shown in \cite{CasDan2019 that the distance sequence of $PK_{n,m}$ is maximal with respect to the partial order $\leq$ among the distance sequences of all connected graphs of order $n$ and size $m$.}}
elements.sent-2411-083: If $I$ is an nondecreasing distance-based topological index, then \begin{equation}I(G) \leq I(PK_{n,m}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-084: If $I$ is a nonincreasing distance-based topological index, then \begin{equation}I(G) \geq I(PK_{n,m}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-085: In this section we consider graphs of given connectivity $\kappa$.
elements.sent-2411-086: While sharp lower bounds for nondecreasing/increasing (or sharp upper bounds for nonincreasing/decreasing) distance-based topological indices of given order and connectivity are known for many distance-based indices, (see \cite{BehJanTae2009 for the hyper-Wiener index, \cite{TomArsJam2015 for the general hyper-Wiener index, and \cite{FenZhoLiu2017 for upper bounds on the Harary index), a sharp upper bound is currently known only for Wiener index.}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: For even $\kappa$, Favaron et al.\ \cite{FavKouMah1989 gave a sharp upper bound, while for odd $\kappa$, the maximum Wiener index was determined asymptotically in \cite{DanMukSwa2009 .}}
elements.sent-2411-088: So far, no upper bounds for hyper-Wiener index or lower bounds on Harary index for graphs of connectivity greater than $1$ appear to be known.
elements.sent-2411-089: Below we give an upper bound on nondecreasing distance-based topological indices for graphs of order $n$ and connectivity $\kappa$, where $\kappa$ is even.
elements.sent-2411-090: In \cite{CasDan2019 it was shown that, for even $\kappa$, the distance sequence of the $\frac{\kappa {2}$-th power of the cycle $C_n$, is maximal among the distance sequences of all $\kappa$-connected graphs of order $n$.}}
elements.sent-2411-091: If $G$ is a $\kappa$-connected graph of order $n$, then \begin{equation}{\cal D}(G) \leq {\cal D}(C_n^{\kappa/2}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-092: As a direct consequence of Lemma \ref{la:cycle-power-dominates-for-given-connectivity and Proposition \ref{prop:application-of-distance-sequence we obtain the following theorem.}}
elements.sent-2411-093: For $\kappa=2$, the extremal graph is unique.
elements.sent-2411-094: In this section we consider maximal outerplanar graphs and a subclass of maximal planar graphs, Apollonian networks, which we define below.
elements.sent-2411-095: We prove our results in a more general setting, for maximal $k$-degenerate graphs, a superclass of $k$-trees.. Let $k\in \mathbb{N}$.
elements.sent-2411-096: A $k$-tree (see \cite{BeiPip1969 ) is a graph defined as follows.}
elements.sent-2411-098: If $G$ is a $k$-tree, then the graph obtained from $G$ by adding a new vertex and joining it to the vertices of a $k$-clique is also a $k$-tree.
elements.sent-2411-100: A graph $G$ is $k$-degenerate if every induced subgraph of $G$ contains a vertex of degree at most $k$ (see \cite{LicWhi1970 ).}
elements.sent-2411-101: A $k$-degenerate graph is maximal $k$-degenerate if after adding any edge it is no longer $k$-degenerate.
elements.sent-2411-102: It was shown in \cite{Bic2012 that every $k$-tree is a maximal $k$-degenerate graph.}
elements.sent-2411-103: It is easy to see that for $k \leq n-1$ the graph $P_{n ^{k}$, i.e., the $k$-th power of the path $P_n$, is a $k$-tree and thus $k$-degenerate.}
elements.sent-2411-104: Bickle and Che \cite{BicChe2021 showed that the graph $P_n^k$ maximises the Wiener index among all maximal $k$-degenerate graphs, and thus among all $k$-trees, of order $n$.}
elements.sent-2411-105: Modifying their proof slightly, we prove that this holds not only for the Wiener index, but for all nondecreasing distance-based topological indices.
elements.sent-2411-106: If $G$ is a maximal $k$-degenerate graph of order $n$, then \begin{equation}{\cal D}(G) \leq {\cal D}(P_n^k).\end{equation}. \end{la.}
elements.sent-2411-108: We prove the statement by induction on $n$.
elements.sent-2411-109: If $n=k+1$, then $G$ is the complete graph $K_{k+1}$, so $G=P_n^{k}$ and the statement holds.
elements.sent-2411-110: Let $n > k+1$ and let $G$ be a maximal $k$-degenerate graph of order $n$.
elements.sent-2411-111: Since $G$ is $k$-degenerate, $G$ contains a vertex $v$ of degree at most $k$.
elements.sent-2411-112: Since $G$ is $k$-connected by Lemma \ref{la:k-degenerate-is-k-connected , it follows that ${\rm deg _G(v)=k$ and that $G-v$ is connected.}}
elements.sent-2411-114: By our induction hypothesis we have \begin{equation}\label{eq:k-tree-1} {\cal D}(G-v) \leq {\cal D}(P_{n-1}^k).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-115: Since $G$ is $k$-connected, there are at least $k$ vertices at distance $i$ from $v$ for $i=1,2,\ldots,{\rm ecc _G(v)-1$.}
elements.sent-2411-117: Let $w$ be an end vertex of the path $P_n$.
elements.sent-2411-118: It is easy to verify that ${\cal D (P_n^k,w) = (1^{(k) , 2^{(k) , \ldots, (s)^{(k) ,(s+1)^{(n-1-sk) )$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-119: Now Lemma \ref{la:sequence-of-G-minus-v in conjunction with \eqref{eq:k-tree-1 and \eqref{eq:k-tree-2 yields \begin{equation}{\cal D}(G) \leq {\cal D}(G-v) \odot {\cal D}_G(v) \leq {\cal D}(P_{n-1}^k) \odot {\cal D}_{P_{n}^k}(w) = {\cal D}(P_{n}^k),\end{equation} as desired.}}}
elements.formula-2411-014: \begin{equation}{\cal D}(G) \leq {\cal D}(G-v) \odot {\cal D}_G(v) \leq {\cal D}(P_{n-1}^k) \odot {\cal D}_{P_{n}^k}(w) = {\cal D}(P_{n}^k),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-120: As a direct consequence of Lemma \ref{la:k-degenerate-is-k-connected and Proposition \ref{prop:application-of-distance-sequence we obtain the following theorem.}}
elements.sent-2411-121: If $I$ is a nondecreasing distance-based topological index, then \begin{equation}I(G) \leq I(P_n^{k}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-122: If $I$ is a nonincreasing distance-based topological index, then \begin{equation}I(G) \geq I(P_n^{k}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-123: It is well-known that every maximal outerplanar graph is a $2$-tree.
elements.sent-2411-124: Among the maximal planar graphs, the planar $3$-trees, also called Apollonian networks, are of interest here.
elements.sent-2411-125: They can be thought of as planar graphs that are obtained from a triangle by successively inserting a new vertex into a face of length three and joining the new vertex to the three vertices on this face by edges.
elements.sent-2411-126: It is easy to verify that the $2$-tree $P_n^2$ is maximal outerplanar, and the $3$-tree $P_n^3$ is an Apollonian network for $n\geq 3$.
elements.sent-2411-127: Hence we have the following corollary.
elements.sent-2411-128: If $I$ is a nondecreasing (nonincreasing) distance-based topological index, then \begin{equation}I(G) \leq I(P_n^{2}) \quad ( I(G) \geq I(P_n^{2})).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-129: If $I$ is a nondecreasing (nonincreasing) distance-based topological index, then \begin{equation}I(G) \leq I(P_n^{3}) \quad ( I(G) \geq I(P_n^{3})).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-130: Also, among all Apollonian networks of order $n$, the graph $P_n^3$ has minimum Harary index and maximum hyper-Wiener index as well as maximum multiplicative Wiener index.
elements.sent-2411-131: Corollary \ref{coro:topololgical-indices-of-maximal-outerplanar-graphs (b) also contains as a special case a sharp upper bound on the Wiener index of Apollonian networks, which was proved by Bickle and Che \cite{BicChe2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-132: We do not know if the conclusion of Corollary \ref{coro:topololgical-indices-of-maximal-outerplanar-graphs (b) holds for all maximal planar graphs.}
elements.sent-2411-133: If this is the case, this would yield, among others, a sharp bound on Harary index and hyper-Wiener index for maximal planar graphs.
elements.sent-2411-134: In this section we consider trees in which every vertex has odd degree.
elements.sent-2411-135: We refer to such trees as odd trees.
elements.sent-2411-136: The minimum and maximum Wiener index of odd trees was determined by \cite{Lin2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-137: For further results on the Wiener index of odd trees see \cite{ForGutLin2013, For2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-138: For other distance-based topological indices, the maximum or minimum value for odd trees appears not to have been investigated.
elements.sent-2411-139: It follows from the handshake lemma that odd trees have even order.
elements.sent-2411-141: Let $S_n$ be the star of order $n$.
elements.sent-2411-142: If $n\geq 6$ define the tree $T_n$ as the tree obtained from a path $P_{n/2+1}$ by attaching a leaf to each internal vertex of the path, and if $n=4$ define $T_n=S_n$.
elements.sent-2411-143: Clearly, $T_n$ is an odd tree of order $n$.
elements.sent-2411-144: We prove the bound on ${\cal D (T)$ by induction on $n$.}
elements.sent-2411-145: If $n=4$, then $T$ equals $S_4$ and the lemma holds.
elements.sent-2411-146: Now assume that or $n\geq 6$, and assume further that the lemma holds for all odd trees of order less than $n$.

elements.sent-2411-148: Let $v_1$ be its end vertex and $w$ the unique neighbour of $v$.
elements.sent-2411-149: Since ${\rm deg _T(w)$ is odd, vertex $w$ has at least one other leaf neighbour $v_2$.}
elements.sent-2411-151: Observe that $T-\{v_1,v_2\}$ is an odd tree.
elements.sent-2411-152: We express ${\cal D (T)$ in terms of ${\cal D (T-\{v_1, v_2\}$ and ${\cal D _{T-v_2 (v_1)$.}}}}

Hence \begin{equation}\label{eq:odd-tree-1} {\cal D}(T) = {\cal D}_{T-\{v_1,v_2\}} \odot {\cal D}_{T-v_2}(v_1) \odot {\cal D}_{T-v_2}(v_1) \odot (2).\end{equation}.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-154: We bound the terms on the right hand side of \eqref{eq:odd-tree-1 separately.}
elements.sent-2411-155: Applying our inductive hypothesis to the odd tree $T-\{v_1,v_2\}$ yields \begin{equation}\label{eq:odd-tree-2} {\cal D}(T-\{v_1, v_2\}) \leq {\cal D}(T_{n-2}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-157: Apart from the neighbour $w$ of $v$ in $T'$, every internal vertex of $T'$ has degree at least $3$.
elements.sent-2411-158: This implies that there are at least two vertices at distance $j$ from $v_1$ for every $j \in \{3,4,\ldots,{\rm ecc _{T-v_2 (v_1)\}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-159: This in turn implies that \begin{equation}\label{eq:odd-tree-3} {\cal D}_{T'}(v_1) \leq (1, 2, 3^{(2)}, 4^{(2)},\ldots,(\frac{n}{2})^{(2)}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-160: Substituting \eqref{eq:odd-tree-2 and \eqref{eq:odd-tree-3 into \eqref{eq:odd-tree-1 , we obtain \begin{eqnarray}{\cal D}(T) \leq {\cal D}(T_{n-2}) \oplus (1, 2, 3^{(2)}, 4^{(2)},\ldots,(\frac{n}{2})^{(2)}) \oplus (1, 2, 3^{(2)}, 4^{(2)},\ldots,(\frac{n}{2})^{(2)}) \odot (2) = {\cal D}(T_{n-2}) \oplus (1^{(2)}, 2^{(3)}, 3^{(4)}, 4^{(4)},\ldots,(\frac{n}{2})^{(4)}).\end{eqnarray}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-161: It is now easy to verify that ${\cal D (T_n) = {\cal D (T_{n-2 ) \oplus (1^{(2) , 2^{(3) , 3^{(4) , 4^{(4) ,\ldots,(\frac{n {2 )^{(4) )$ for $n\geq 6$.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-162: Hence we obtain that ${\cal D (T) \leq {\cal D (T_{n )$, as desired.}}}
elements.sent-2411-163: As a direct consequence of Lemma \ref{la:odd-trees and Proposition \ref{prop:application-of-distance-sequence we obtain the following theorem.}}
elements.sent-2411-164: If $I$ is a nondecreasing distance-based topological index, then \begin{equation}I(G) \leq I(T_n).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-165: If $I$ is a nonincreasing distance-based topological index, then \begin{equation}I(G) \geq I(T_n).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-166: The approach taken in this paper, to bound distance-based topological indices by considering the distance sequence appears very fruitful.
elements.sent-2411-167: It would be useful to find further graph classes in which a similar approach works.
